6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

By Adebowale Adegbenjo | Digital13Media
African travel agencies using AI-generated content are becoming harder to find in both traditional search and AI-generated recommendations, not because AI content is low quality, but because it is indistinguishable from every other agency’s content. The fix is not avoiding AI. It is using AI earlier in the creative process while preserving market-specific voice, cultural context, and the kind of insider knowledge that no language model was trained on.
Scroll through the Instagram pages of ten Nigerian travel agencies this week.
Count how many look identical.
Same oversaturated beach photo. Same bold sans-serif font dropped over a sunset. Same “Book Now” call to action sitting in the same bottom-right corner. The brand colors might shift; one agency uses deep blue, another goes with burnt orange, but the underlying visual grammar is the same document, differently dressed.
That’s the design problem. And it’s visible enough that a keen eye catches it within three seconds.
The content problem is quieter. And it is doing far more damage.
What AI Content Actually Produces, And Why It Fails African Markets Specifically
When a travel agency in Lagos, Abuja, or Port Harcourt opens ChatGPT and asks it to write a destination guide for, say, a corporate retreat in Zanzibar, here is what happens:
The model reaches into its training data, billions of words scraped from the internet, the overwhelming majority of which were written by and for Western travelers, Western corporate travel managers, and Western content teams working for Western travel brands.
The output is grammatically clean. It reads professionally. It might even rank for a generic keyword.
But it doesn’t sound like an agency that has navigated the complexity of West African corporate travel. It doesn’t reference the BSP reconciliation headaches that every Nigerian TMC MD knows by name. It doesn’t acknowledge that the “seamless booking experience” it promises is being offered by an agency whose clients have spent years battling FOREX restrictions, delayed visa approvals, and international airlines that still treat Lagos as an afterthought on their route maps.
The content is technically competent. Culturally, it is a stranger.
And AI-powered search engines, Perplexity, Google’s AI Overviews, and ChatGPT’s browse feature are increasingly good at detecting that strangeness. Not consciously. But structurally. They cite content that carries signals of genuine expertise: specific named experience, original data, contextual depth, and the kind of granular market knowledge that cannot be statistically averaged from generic internet text.
Generic AI content carries none of those signals.
The Visibility Cliff No One Is Talking About
Here is what makes this worse than it looks on the surface.
Ten years ago, an African travel agency with a decent website and consistent social media presence could build visibility through sheer volume. Post regularly. Show up. Be seen.
That window is closing.
The way people discover travel services is shifting from “I’ll Google it and scroll through results” to “I’ll ask an AI and act on what it recommends.” Perplexity is processing over 100 million queries per month. Google’s AI Mode is now the default entry point for a growing percentage of search traffic. ChatGPT’s search feature, once a novelty, is now the starting point for millions of business travel decisions globally.
In this environment, content that cannot be cited does not exist.
And content built from the same AI tools, using the same prompts, trained on the same data, producing the same structure, will not be cited. Not because the algorithm dislikes it. Because there is nothing in it that a generative engine could use to differentiate one agency from another.
When every agency sounds identical, the algorithm stops choosing between them. It chooses the sources that sound authoritative, specific, and earned, and those sources are rarely the agencies themselves.
The Design Problem Is a Preview of the Content Crisis
Back to those Instagram pages for a moment.
The design problem you spotted, brand colors layered over AI-generated visual templates, differentiated only at the surface, is not a design failure. It is a content strategy failure wearing designer clothes.
What those agencies are actually saying is, “We know AI tools exist, and we are using them to produce output faster.”
That instinct is correct.
The execution is where it breaks down.
When AI tools are used at the output stage to generate the final flyer, the final caption, or the final destination guide, the result is always average. Because that is what language models and image models are optimized to produce: the statistical center of all the content they were trained on. Not the outlier. Not specific. The average.
The agencies pulling ahead in AI-search visibility are not the ones avoiding these tools. They are the ones who understand where in the creative process those tools belong.
Where AI Actually Belongs in a Travel Agency’s Content Process
Think of content production as having two distinct phases.
The first phase is divergence, generating ideas, angles, questions, frameworks, and possibilities. This is where AI is genuinely powerful. A content team that uses AI to brainstorm twenty different angles on a corporate travel policy guide, then selects the two that actually reflect their market experience, is using the technology correctly.
The second phase is convergence, producing the final content that goes out under the agency’s name. This is where human taste, market specificity, and insider knowledge have to take over.
Not because AI cannot write the words. It can.
But because the words it writes in convergence mode will be indistinguishable from the words it writes for every other agency asking the same prompt. And in an AI search environment, “indistinguishable” means “invisible.”
The practical framework looks like this:
Use AI to expand. Generate the question clusters your corporate clients are actually asking. Map the topics that surround your core service. Build the structural skeleton of a pillar content piece.
Use human knowledge to fill. What does a corporate travel manager at a Lagos-based multinational actually need to know when booking a multi-leg West African itinerary? What goes wrong with business travel across ECOWAS routes that no generic content platform will ever tell them? That knowledge exists in your agency. It does not exist in any AI training dataset.
Use AI again to refine. Grammar. Structure. SEO tagging. Schema markup for GEO citation. These are mechanical tasks. Let the tools handle mechanics.
The human layer, the one that makes the content citable, credible, and African-market-specific, sits in the middle. It cannot be outsourced. It is the only part that makes your agency distinct.
The Question Worth Sitting With
Most Nigerian travel agencies are not losing visibility because they are doing too little.
They are losing it because they are doing the right thing at the wrong stage.
The agency that uses AI to produce forty posts a month is not building a content engine. It is building a content noise machine, one that trains both human audiences and AI systems to scroll past, skip over, and ultimately forget the brand entirely.
The agency that produces eight pieces a month, each one carrying specific market insight, named expertise, and genuine cultural context, is building something that compounds.
There is a version of your agency that ChatGPT recommends by name to a procurement manager who has never heard of you.
Getting there is not about posting more.
It is about saying something that only you could say.
Adebowale Adegbenjo is a GEO content strategist and the founder of Digital13Media, a Lagos-based consultancy that builds content systems for African travel agencies, TMCs, and hospitality brands targeting corporate clients. His work focuses on making African travel expertise visible in AI-generated search environments.
Want to know how your agency’s current content scores for AI citability? Follow Adebowale on LinkedIn for the weekly audit framework, or send a DM with the word AUDIT to get the checklist.
